title: Federated chat across multiple servers with deduped delivery
business_context: Matrix-style federated chat: rooms exist across multiple home servers. Users on server-A talking to users on server-B. Messages must arrive once, in order, across federation. Scale: 100 federated servers, 1M federated rooms. If wrong: messages duplicate or arrive out of order → chat unusable for cross-org collab.
constraints: Federation event log per room with monotonic depth
Local users see messages instantly even if remote server slow
Conflict-free resolution of out-of-order federation events
failure_modes_to_handle: Federation event arrives out of order (depth N+2 before N+1)
Server B down for 24h then comes back with backlog
Replay attack: same event_id submitted twice
architecture_reasoning:
1. Each home server stores rooms it participates in. Messages have (event_id, depth, prev_events[], room_id). Depth is server-side monotonic per-room.
2. Local send: write to PG, increment room depth, push to local room subscribers, enqueue federation jobs for each remote server.
3. Federation receive: validate event_id dedup, check prev_events present (state resolution), insert with depth, broadcast to local subscribers.
4. Missing prev_events: backfill request to source server.
5. Mongo for user profiles + room metadata (per-server flexibility). PG for events log (linear ordering critical).
6. Adapter: Redis per home server.
7. Rejected: Kafka-style cross-server log — overkill; HTTP push with retry sufficient at this scale.

database_design.indexes: room_events PK is the dedup key. (room_id, depth) for ordered fetch + backfill detection. federation_queue (dest_server, delivered, next_try_at) for sender worker.
database_design.queries: Send local: INSERT room_events ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING + UPDATE rooms.max_depth=GREATEST(max_depth, $depth). Receive remote: same INSERT + check prev_events all present. Backfill: SELECT WHERE event_id = ANY($missing).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Send tx: SELECT max_depth FROR UPDATE → compute depth = max+1 → INSERT event → UPDATE max_depth → INSERT federation_queue rows for each member server → COMMIT. Idempotency via event_id PK.

socket_io_design.events: type FedEvent = { eventId:string; roomId:string; depth:number; sender:string; type:string; content:any; originServer:string; ts:number }; Events: 'room:join' (C→S ack with recent events + maxDepth), 'msg:send' (C→S), 'event' (S→C local broadcast), 'backfill' (C→S request range).

race_conditions_addressed: Duplicate federation event: event_id PK INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.
Out-of-order arrival (depth N+2 before N+1): inserted anyway with prev_events check; missing prev triggers backfill; client sorts by depth.
Server down 24h: federation_queue retries with backoff; on dest recovery, queue drains.
Replay attack: event_id includes origin server prefix; PK rejects.
Clock skew: depth is per-server monotonic, NOT timestamp-based; origin_ts shown to users but server uses depth for ordering.
scaling_considerations: 10x (1000 servers, 10M rooms): federation_queue grows — partition by dest_server hash; per-dest worker pool. 100x: switch to streaming federation (like Matrix /sync) instead of per-event push.
observability: Metrics: federation_lag_per_server (now - oldest pending), backfill_request_rate, dedup_rate. Logs: every federation send/receive with eventId, dest. Alerts: federation_lag > 5min for any server, backfill_rate > 10% (state divergence).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: each server keeps own messages, send via HTTP to other servers. Breaks: (1) no dedup = duplicate messages; (2) no ordering = chaos; (3) no retry = lost messages on transient failure; (4) no backfill = forks. Killer bug: server B comes back after 24h outage, dumps 100k backlog events in random order — without depth + prev_events, the timeline is unrecoverable.

title: Live A/B test variant assignment with conversion stream
business_context: Product analytics: users assigned to A/B variants, conversions tracked live. PM watches dashboard: variant counts + conversion rate + statistical significance updating live. Scale: 50 active experiments, 10M user assignments, 1k conversions/sec. If wrong: PM ships losing variant.
failure_modes_to_handle: Experiment ended but old assignments keep converting
Stat library numerical issues at extreme proportions
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for experiments + assignments + conversions. Materialized view for live counts; refresh every 10s.\n2. Conversion event: insert + UPDATE counts cache.\n3. Stat sig computed by worker every 30s.

database_design.queries: Refresh mv: REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY variant_stats. Stat sig: compute z-test on the rows.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Conversion insert + cache UPDATE in single tx.

race_conditions_addressed: Sticky reassignment: PK ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE returns original variant.
Conversion before assignment: rejected as 'not_assigned'; client must call assign first.
Ended experiment: variants left in place; conversions continue to attribute correctly via PK lookup.
Stat sig numerical: ztest2prop uses Wilson interval to avoid divide-by-zero at extreme p.
scaling_considerations: 10x: REFRESH CONCURRENTLY blocks none; mv refresh time grows with assignments — switch to incremental aggregation via per-variant counter table updated by triggers.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: client-side variant selection. Breaks: stickiness lost on reload; PM can't trust numbers.

title: MMO chat with proximity, channel, and party — multi-room composition
business_context: MMORPG chat: per-zone chat (proximity), global trade channel, party chat. Player in zone sees zone chat from nearby players only. Scale: 50 zones × 1k players each = 50k concurrent. If wrong: chat spam from far away, party messages leak.
constraints: Multi-room subscription per player (zone + party + global)
Zone room dynamic on player movement
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for player chat metadata + mute + audit.\n2. Socket rooms: zone:{id}, party:{id}, global:trade.\n3. Rate limits per channel via Redis token bucket.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE chat_logs (id BIGSERIAL PK, channel TEXT, channel_key TEXT, sender_id BIGINT, body TEXT, ts TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());\nCREATE INDEX ON chat_logs (channel, channel_key, ts);\nCREATE TABLE mutes (muter_id BIGINT, muted_id BIGINT, PRIMARY KEY (muter_id, muted_id));
database_design.queries: Insert + emit. Mute check on receive client-side (server filters too).

race_conditions_addressed: Zone hop mid-message: socket.to room is computed at emit; once joined, broadcast goes to right room.
Party disband mid-message: target may not exist — emit just goes to empty room, no harm.
Mute: server-side filter via mutes table check (omitted for brevity); client-side dedup defense in depth.
Spam: token bucket per channel per user.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-zone room counts can be huge — sharding by zone hash; consider zone-affinity routing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: broadcast all messages to all. Breaks: bus saturation, privacy violation (party chat leaks).

title: Dutch auction with descending price tick and first-bidder-wins
business_context: Dutch auction (price descends until someone bids). Used for IPO/limited drops. Each tick (1s) price drops by step. First socket to submit valid bid wins atomically. Scale: 1000 viewers per drop.
constraints: Tick visible to all viewers within 200ms
Bidder rejected if amount mismatches current tick
failure_modes_to_handle: Two bids arrive at same millisecond
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo for auction state (price, status). Tick updates server-side every second. First bid that wins atomic via findOneAndUpdate with status='live' guard.\n2. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: const DutchSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, itemId: String, startPriceCents: Number, floorPriceCents: Number, stepCents: Number, currentPriceCents: Number, status: { type: String, enum: ['scheduled','live','won','expired'] }, winnerUserId: String, wonAtCents: Number, wonAt: Date, startedAt: Date });
database_design.queries: Tick: findOneAndUpdate inc/dec price with status check. Bid: atomic claim — findOneAndUpdate with status='live' set status='won'.

race_conditions_addressed: Same-ms double bid: findOneAndUpdate with status='live' guard ensures atomic claim; only one transitions to 'won'.
Tick worker death: setInterval idempotent; new instance picks up.
Late bid: status='live' filter rejects.
Bid spam: rate-limit middleware drops; failed bids don't change state.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-auction findOneAndUpdate is O(1) keyed by _id.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: client-side timer + check-then-act. Breaks: race = double winner.

title: Help-desk agent presence with skill-based routing and concurrent-chat cap
business_context: Customer support: agents show as available/busy. Incoming chat routed to least-busy agent with matching skill (English+Billing). Cap: 3 concurrent chats per agent. Scale: 500 agents, 5k chats/day. If wrong: customers wait, agents overloaded.
constraints: Failover when agent goes offline mid-chat
failure_modes_to_handle: Two routing decisions for same chat
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for agents + chats. Redis for hot presence + per-skill queue.\n2. On chat arrival: pick agent via SELECT FOR UPDATE skipping busy.\n3. On agent disconnect: chats reassigned via reroute job.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE agents (id BIGSERIAL PK, name TEXT, skills TEXT[], status TEXT, max_concurrent INT DEFAULT 3, current_load INT DEFAULT 0);\nCREATE INDEX ON agents (status, current_load) WHERE status='available';\nCREATE TABLE chats (id BIGSERIAL PK, customer_id BIGINT, required_skills TEXT[], assigned_agent_id BIGINT REFERENCES agents(id), status TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, closed_at TIMESTAMPTZ);
database_design.queries: Route: SELECT FROM agents WHERE status='available' AND skills @> $1 AND current_load < max_concurrent ORDER BY current_load ASC LIMIT 1 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Routing tx: SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED agent → UPDATE current_load+1 → UPDATE chat assigned_agent_id.

race_conditions_addressed: Two routes same chat: SKIP LOCKED ensures different agents picked per concurrent tryRoute calls.
Agent disconnect with 3 chats: re-route loop pickups; if no available agent, back to queue.
Skill set change: next routing uses fresh agents.skills.
Customer abandon: client emits chat:cancel → mark chat closed.
scaling_considerations: 10x: routing query O(log n) with skills GIN; queue per skill set partitioned.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: pick random available agent. Breaks: load imbalance, skill mismatch.

title: Suggesting mode for docs: track-changes review with accept/reject
business_context: Doc editor 'Suggesting mode' (Google Docs style). Edits show as suggestions; collaborators accept/reject. Original author sees inline diff. Scale: 100k docs in review.
constraints: Suggestions don't mutate base doc until accepted
Author can accept/reject individually or all
Reviewer can edit their own suggestion
Conflict: two suggestions on same range
failure_modes_to_handle: Accept while another reviewer is editing same suggestion
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo: suggestions are separate collection referencing doc + block + range.\n2. Acceptance applies suggestion as an op (sock-001) bumping doc version.\n3. Anchor uses block_id + char offset; remapped on base edits via simple op-transform.

database_design.schema: const SuggestionSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, docId: String, blockId: String, range: { start: Number, end: Number }, kind: { type: String, enum: ['insert','delete','replace'] }, content: String, authorId: String, status: { type: String, enum: ['open','accepted','rejected','superseded'], default: 'open' }, baseVersion: Number, createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now } });\nSuggestionSchema.index({ docId: 1, status: 1 });
database_design.queries: Open suggestions: find({docId, status: 'open'}). Apply: update doc + suggestion.status='accepted' in tx.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Mongo tx for accept: status check → apply as doc op → set accepted.

race_conditions_addressed: Two reviewers accept same suggestion: findOneAndUpdate with status='open' guard — second sees 'already_handled'.
Bulk accept: apply one-by-one with version increment; each accept observes prior accepts via fresh block read.
Anchor drift: baseVersion stored; if block.version != baseVersion at accept time, mark superseded and re-prompt author.
scaling_considerations: 10x: bulk accept = N tx; batch into single tx with multi-op apply for performance.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: apply suggestion immediately as edit. Breaks: no review semantics; author can't reject.

title: Webhook delivery with HMAC sign and retry-after honoring
business_context: SaaS webhook system: customers register endpoints to receive event notifications. Deliveries signed with HMAC; honor Retry-After header. Scale: 10k webhooks, 100k events/hour.
constraints: Delivery status visible live to customer dashboard
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for webhooks + deliveries log. BullMQ for delivery scheduling with backoff.\n2. Socket for live dashboard updates.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE webhooks (id BIGSERIAL PK, customer_id BIGINT, url TEXT, secret TEXT, enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT true);\nCREATE TABLE webhook_deliveries (id BIGSERIAL PK, webhook_id BIGINT, event_id TEXT, payload JSONB, status TEXT, attempts INT DEFAULT 0, next_try_at TIMESTAMPTZ, response_code INT, response_body TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ);\nCREATE INDEX ON webhook_deliveries (webhook_id, created_at DESC);
database_design.queries: Enqueue + status updates.

race_conditions_addressed: Secret rotation mid-flight: worker reads secret per attempt — uses latest.
Endpoint slow: timeout cancels, increment attempt, schedule retry.
Customer suspended: enabled=false skips on dispatch; in-flight deliveries finish.
Same delivery retried twice: jobId per delivery is unique; BullMQ dedup.
scaling_considerations: 10x: Worker concurrency tuned to outbound HTTP budget.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: fire-and-forget HTTP. Breaks: customer never knows delivery status; no retries.

title: Energy meter rollup with tiered TimescaleDB compression and live tile
business_context: Building energy management: 500 sub-meters at 1Hz each. Live tile shows current kW; historical chart shows 24h. Storage cost matters — compression after 1h, downsample after 7d. Scale: 10k buildings.
architecture_reasoning: 1. TimescaleDB hypertable for samples. Continuous aggregates for 1min/1h. Compression policy after 1h. Drop chunks older than 90d.\n2. Live socket: tail latest sample via worker subscribed to PG LISTEN on insert trigger.

database_design.queries: Live tile: SELECT meter_id, kw FROM meter_samples WHERE building_id=$1 AND ts > now()-interval '5 seconds' ORDER BY ts DESC. Chart: SELECT FROM meter_1m WHERE building_id=$1 AND bucket > now()-interval '24h'.

race_conditions_addressed: Negative readings: ingest validation rejects; alert raised.
Tenant deletion: cascade drop on building; chunks GC'd by retention policy.
CAgg lag: live tile reads raw hypertable (hot chunk), not cagg.
scaling_considerations: 10x: compression + retention bounds disk. 100x: per-region cluster.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: regular table. Breaks: 90d × 500 meters × 1Hz = 4B rows, 100GB+ disk.

title: PSTN bridge: SIP gateway in/out with call control via socket
business_context: VoIP product: dial out to PSTN via SIP gateway; receive inbound calls routed to web agents. Socket controls (mute/hold/transfer). Scale: 500 simul calls.
constraints: Call state synced across web UI and SIP gateway
Reconnect web UI mid-call doesn't drop SIP leg
failure_modes_to_handle: Two control commands race (mute then unmute fast)
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for calls + leg state. SIP gateway exposes HTTP control API; socket bridges UI commands.\n2. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE calls (id BIGSERIAL PK, agent_id BIGINT, caller_e164 TEXT, callee_e164 TEXT, direction TEXT, status TEXT, sip_call_id TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ, duration_sec INT, cost_cents BIGINT);\nCREATE INDEX ON calls (agent_id, started_at DESC);\nCREATE TABLE call_events (id BIGSERIAL PK, call_id BIGINT, event TEXT, payload JSONB, ts TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());
database_design.queries: State transitions + event log.

race_conditions_addressed: Two control commands fast: SIP gateway serializes per call_id; second arrives after first.
Web disconnect mid-call: SIP leg persists; reconnect rejoins call room and resumes state via subscribe.
Gateway disconnect: socket emits 'call:ended' with reason; UI prompts retry.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-call control rate low; scale by SIP gateway pool.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: store call state in JS. Breaks: server restart loses calls; web disconnect drops SIP leg.

title: Approval workflow with sequential signers and live signature receipt
business_context: E-signature: 5 signers in sequence; document locked while signer signs; live status to all parties. Scale: 100k docs in flight.
constraints: Live status visible to all parties
Audit trail of every state change
failure_modes_to_handle: Two signers attempt out of order
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for workflow state, signer order, signatures (audit-grade). Mongo for doc metadata + signature visuals.\n2. Sequential lock via order_index check.\n3. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE workflows (id BIGSERIAL PK, doc_id TEXT, status TEXT, current_signer_idx INT DEFAULT 0, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ);\nCREATE TABLE signers (workflow_id BIGINT, order_idx INT, user_id BIGINT, signature_b64 TEXT, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, declined_at TIMESTAMPTZ, PRIMARY KEY (workflow_id, order_idx));\nCREATE TABLE workflow_events (id BIGSERIAL PK, workflow_id BIGINT, type TEXT, actor_id BIGINT, payload JSONB, ts TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());
database_design.queries: Sign: UPDATE WHERE order_idx = current AND user_id = sig user.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Sign tx: UPDATE signer + increment workflow.current_signer_idx + insert event.

race_conditions_addressed: Out-of-order sign: tx FOR UPDATE + idx check.
Disconnect mid-sign: no state change persisted until COMMIT; user retries.
Withdraw mid-flow: workflow status → 'withdrawn' guards future signs.
Invalid sig: verifySignature rejects before any persistence.
scaling_considerations: Low contention per workflow (sequential by nature).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: parallel sign. Breaks: out-of-order signatures invalidate doc.

title: Distributed map-reduce progress with shard-level reporting
business_context: Data pipeline: 100 shards, each runs map task. UI shows per-shard progress + aggregated reduce result. Scale: 50 simul jobs.
failure_modes_to_handle: Reduce error after all shards done
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo for job + per-shard docs. Workers update shard docs; aggregation listens to change stream.

database_design.schema: const JobSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, type: String, totalShards: Number, completedShards: Number, status: String, result: Schema.Types.Mixed, createdAt: Date, completedAt: Date });\nconst ShardSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, jobId: { type: String, index: true }, idx: Number, status: String, progress: Number, partialResult: Schema.Types.Mixed, startedAt: Date, endedAt: Date });
database_design.queries: Update shard + check completion in worker.

race_conditions_addressed: Two workers complete simultaneously: $inc atomic; completedShards == total triggers single transition.
Cancel mid-run: shards poll job.status; abort own work.
Slow straggler: progress still emits; completion eventual.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-shard updates fan-out — throttle progress emits at 1Hz.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: keep counters in memory. Breaks: worker crash loses progress; no resume.

title: Database query editor with live SQL syntax-check and shared explain output
business_context: DBA collaborative SQL workbench: 2-3 DBAs iterate on query; live syntax check; shared EXPLAIN run. Scale: 200 DBAs, 50 hot sessions.
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for sessions + queries + explain results. Sandboxed PG instance for actual queries (separate from production).

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE sql_sessions (id BIGSERIAL PK, owner_id BIGINT, name TEXT, current_query TEXT, version INT DEFAULT 0, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());\nCREATE TABLE sql_runs (id BIGSERIAL PK, session_id BIGINT, runner_id BIGINT, query TEXT, explain_json JSONB, duration_ms INT, status TEXT, ran_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());
database_design.queries: Append run after EXPLAIN.

race_conditions_addressed: DDL attempt: regex pre-check; sandboxPool also runs as read-only role.
Two runs concurrent: each gets own run row; no contention.
Timeout: sandbox sets statement_timeout=10000.
Peer edit overwrites: last-write-wins; version persisted for audit.
scaling_considerations: 10x: sandbox pool autoscale; per-runner DBA isolation via SET ROLE.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: run on prod. Disaster waiting.

title: AI-assistant chat with token-streaming response
business_context: LLM chat UI: assistant tokens stream as they arrive from model API. User can cancel mid-stream. Scale: 1000 concurrent conversations.
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo for conversation log. Upstream LLM via streaming API; pipe tokens through socket.\n2. Cancel via AbortController.

database_design.schema: const ConvoSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, userId: String, messages: [{ role: String, content: String, tokens: Number, createdAt: Date, finishReason: String }], createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now } });
database_design.queries: Append message after completion.

race_conditions_addressed: Multiple cancel: AbortController abort idempotent.
Disconnect mid-stream: disconnect handler aborts all in-flight, no orphan upstream cost.
Tokens out-of-order: SDK guarantees order within a stream.
Restart resume: not supported here — restart = lost stream; client retries from last persisted message.
scaling_considerations: 10x: upstream API rate-limit pooling.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: await full response then emit. Breaks: 30s wait + no perceived progress; user thinks app froze.

title: Async turn-based game (Words With Friends) with push notification on opponent's move
business_context: Async game: players take turn whenever (could be hours apart). Push notification when opponent moves. Scale: 1M active matches.
failure_modes_to_handle: Both players try to move same turn
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for matches + moves (transactional state machine).\n2. On move commit: enqueue push to opponent via sock-045.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE async_matches (id BIGSERIAL PK, p1 BIGINT, p2 BIGINT, current_turn TEXT, state JSONB, status TEXT, last_move_at TIMESTAMPTZ, abandoned_at TIMESTAMPTZ);\nCREATE TABLE async_moves (id BIGSERIAL PK, match_id BIGINT, player_id BIGINT, move JSONB, at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());
database_design.queries: Submit move: tx with turn check.

race_conditions_addressed: Out-of-turn: FOR UPDATE + turn check.
Both submit: serialized by lock.
Abandoned: sweeper sets status='abandoned' after 14d.
Push fail: dispatchPush has its own retry; move already persisted.
scaling_considerations: Per-match contention low.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: trust client move. Breaks: cheaters.

title: Auction sniping prevention with anti-snipe extension and watcher count
business_context: eBay-style auction. Bid in last 30s extends end by 5min (anti-snipe). Watcher count visible. Scale: 100k active auctions.
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for auctions + bids. On bid: if (now > end - 30s) → end = now + 5min.\n2. Server time authoritative.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE eb_auctions (id BIGSERIAL PK, item_id BIGINT, current_bid_cents BIGINT DEFAULT 0, current_bidder_id BIGINT, ends_at TIMESTAMPTZ, reserve_cents BIGINT, reserve_met BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, status TEXT DEFAULT 'live');\nCREATE TABLE eb_bids (id BIGSERIAL PK, auction_id BIGINT, bidder_id BIGINT, amount_cents BIGINT, ts TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());\nCREATE INDEX ON eb_bids (auction_id, id DESC);
database_design.queries: Bid: tx with FOR UPDATE.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Bid tx: SELECT FOR UPDATE → compare current → INSERT bid → UPDATE end if last 30s.

race_conditions_addressed: Bid at end: server time check inside lock.
Concurrent bids extend: each tx reads fresh ends_at; both extend if both in last 30s.
Sniper clock skew: server time authoritative; client clock irrelevant.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-auction advisory lock fine.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: client computes end. Sniper sets clock back, wins everything.

title: Sales-team activity feed showing who's in which CRM record live
business_context: Sales CRM: see which rep is viewing/editing which deal in real time. Prevent stepping on each other. Scale: 5k reps, 100k deals.
constraints: Editing intent flagged distinct from viewing
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for deals + visibility rules. Redis for hot presence per deal.\n2. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE crm_visibility (viewer_id BIGINT, target_role TEXT, visible BOOLEAN, PRIMARY KEY (viewer_id, target_role));\n// Redis: HASH deal:viewers:{dealId} → repId → {ts, intent: 'view'|'edit'}, EX 60
database_design.queries: Get visible viewers: HGETALL then filter by viewer role rules.

race_conditions_addressed: Two edit intents: both flagged; UI shows warning.
Tab close: server-side TTL + disconnect handler.
Privacy: per-viewer filter on emit; junior never sees senior.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-viewer filter is expensive — precompute role visibility set; cache.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: broadcast all viewers. Privacy leak.

title: Image gallery with pin annotations and crop-aware coords
business_context: Architectural plan review: pin comments on images. Pin must follow crop/rotate transforms. Scale: 50k images, 20 reviewers.
constraints: Pin coords in normalized image space
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo for pins. PG for image registry + versions.

database_design.queries: Pin fetch: find({imageId, imageVersion, resolved: false}).

race_conditions_addressed: Image replaced: imageVersion filter; new version starts fresh; old pins archived.
Two move concurrent: last update wins (no version on pin; pins are inexpensive).
Resolve race: WHERE resolved=false ensures atomic.
Deleted region: client-side check; pin shows 'orphan' badge.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-image pin count small (<100); thumbnail count denormalized via $size aggregation cached.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: pixel coords. Breaks: image scaling = pins drift.

title: In-app banner alert with broadcast targeting and dismissal sync
business_context: Product banner: 'maintenance window tonight'. Targeted by region, plan, role. Dismissed once stays dismissed. Scale: 1M users.
constraints: Targeting rules complex (region X AND plan Y)
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for banners + targeting + dismissals. Pre-compute affected user set via materialized view periodically.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE banners (id BIGSERIAL PK, title TEXT, body TEXT, target_query JSONB, starts_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ends_at TIMESTAMPTZ);\nCREATE TABLE banner_dismissals (user_id BIGINT, banner_id BIGINT, dismissed_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), PRIMARY KEY (user_id, banner_id));
database_design.queries: Active banners for user: SELECT b FROM banners b WHERE now() BETWEEN starts_at AND ends_at AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM dismissals WHERE user_id=$1 AND banner_id=b.id) AND matches_target(b.target_query, $user).

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'banner:subscribe' (ack with active), 'banner:show' (S→C), 'banner:dismiss' (C→S), 'banner:hidden' (S→C broadcast for sync).

race_conditions_addressed: Tab race: 'banner:hidden' broadcast to user:{id} room hits all tabs.
Targeting misconfig: matches_target server-side; mistake hits all eligible but at least dedup via dismissals.
Mass blast: chunked emits per 1k users.
scaling_considerations: 10x: enumeration query expensive — pre-compute via materialized view + refresh.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: localStorage dismiss. Breaks: cross-device, cross-browser inconsistency.

title: Camera-feed metadata fan-out (detected objects per frame)
business_context: CCTV/AI: 200 cameras, on-device YOLO sends bounding-box JSON at 5Hz per camera. Live UI overlays boxes on video. Scale: 50 viewers per camera.
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo time-series for detections. PG for camera registry.\n2. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: const DetectionSchema = new Schema({ cameraId: String, frameTs: Date, objects: [{ class: String, conf: Number, bbox: [Number] }] }, { timeseries: { timeField: 'frameTs', metaField: 'cameraId', granularity: 'seconds' } });\nDetectionSchema.index({ 'objects.class': 1, frameTs: -1 });
database_design.queries: Live broadcast direct from ingest. History: find by cameraId + frameTs + class.

race_conditions_addressed: Out-of-order frames: client renders latest by frameTs.
Camera disconnect: no new ingest; client can detect via stale timer.
High frame rate: ingester back-pressure; drop oldest in buffer.
scaling_considerations: 10x: time-series collection grows fast — drop chunks > 30d.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: broadcast every detection to everyone. Bandwidth explosion.

title: Hybrid call: P2P for ≤4, SFU upgrade at 5+
business_context: Video chat starts P2P (cheap) but upgrades to SFU when 5th joins (P2P mesh too costly). Smooth transition with no media interruption. Scale: 10k concurrent meetings.
failure_modes_to_handle: SFU not available — fall back to P2P + cap participants
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo for room state including mode. PG for cost tracking.\n2. Switch trigger: participant count crosses 4↔5. Server emits 'mode:switch' → clients establish new peer/sfu connections, then close old.

database_design.queries: Atomic count increment.

socket_io_design.events: Events: 'room:join' (ack with mode+peers), 'sdp', 'ice', 'mode:switch' (S→C with target mode + transports), 'mode:switched' (C→S ack), 'room:state'.

race_conditions_addressed: 5th and 6th join same instant: $push with array size check; one of them triggers switchToSfu; second sees mode already 'sfu' and proceeds.
SFU unavailable: graceful degradation event; UI shows 'too full, retry'.
Mid-switch leave: room.participants count drops mid-process; switch completes anyway, room operates on new mode.
scaling_considerations: 10x: SFU transport provisioning is cost — per-region SFU pool.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: P2P only. Breaks: at 5+ users, mesh = 10 connections, bandwidth O(n²).

title: Spreadsheet-style time-tracking sheet with bulk-fill and validation
business_context: Time tracking: weekly grid of (project × day). Bulk-fill via drag. Validation (total ≤ 40h/week). Multi-user co-edit in same sheet (team manager edits own rows). Scale: 50k users.
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG with row-per-cell. Bulk-fill = single tx upsert many cells.\n2. Validation tx around any submit.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE time_cells (sheet_id BIGINT, project_id BIGINT, day DATE, hours NUMERIC, edited_by BIGINT, edited_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), PRIMARY KEY (sheet_id, project_id, day));\nCREATE TABLE time_sheets (id BIGSERIAL PK, user_id BIGINT, week_start DATE, status TEXT, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ);
database_design.queries: Bulk: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE.

race_conditions_addressed: Bulk-fill vs peer edit: advisory lock serializes per sheet.
Submit race: WHERE status='draft' guard.
Validation rule change: each tx runs current rules.
Total recomputed on every tx — guaranteed consistent.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-sheet lock fine.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: per-cell HTTP PATCH. Breaks: bulk fill of 200 cells = 200 round trips.

title: Streaming export: query → S3 → email link, with progress
business_context: Analytics export: user requests CSV of millions of rows. Server streams query result to S3, emails link when done. Live progress in UI. Scale: 100 simul exports.
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for export jobs + progress. Mongo for query metadata (flexible filter shapes).\n2. Worker streams PG cursor → CSV → multipart S3 upload.\n3. Socket for live progress.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE exports (id BIGSERIAL PK, user_id BIGINT, query_hash TEXT, total_rows BIGINT, processed_rows BIGINT DEFAULT 0, status TEXT, s3_key TEXT, s3_upload_id TEXT, parts JSONB DEFAULT '[]', created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ);\n// Mongo: export_queries\nconst ExportQuerySchema = new Schema({ exportId: String, filter: Schema.Types.Mixed, sort: Schema.Types.Mixed, fields: [String] });
database_design.queries: Streaming cursor SELECT ... FROM big_table WHERE ...
database_design.transaction_strategy: None — append to parts list as multipart upload progresses.

race_conditions_addressed: Worker dies: parts persisted in PG; restart resumes from last part + processed_rows offset.
Cancel mid-upload: abortMultipartUpload cleans S3.
S3 part failure: retry inside worker; if persistent, mark failed.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-export memory bounded by part size; concurrent worker pool tuned by S3 bandwidth.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: buffer all rows then PUT. Breaks: OOM on 10M rows.

title: Mind-map collaborative editing with sibling reordering
business_context: MindMap app: tree of nodes; users add/move/reparent. Sibling order matters. Scale: 10k maps × 5 collaborators.
architecture_reasoning: 1. Mongo nodes with parentId + order. Cycle detection on reparent via walk.\n2. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: const MapNodeSchema = new Schema({ _id: String, mapId: String, parentId: String, order: Number, text: String, version: { type: Number, default: 1 } });\nMapNodeSchema.index({ mapId: 1, parentId: 1, order: 1 });
database_design.queries: Reorder: bulkWrite of $set order. Reparent: check cycle then $set parentId.

race_conditions_addressed: Cycle: tx walk detects; abort.
Concurrent reorder: bulkWrite atomic; second reorder wins.
Subtree delete: traversal in single op; broadcast collects all deleted ids.
scaling_considerations: 10x: tree walks expensive at 10k nodes — cache subtree paths.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: trust client cycle check. Breaks: malicious or buggy clients create cycles.

title: Customer-support chat with canned responses and live translation
business_context: Support chat in 20 languages. Agent sees customer message in original + translated. Canned responses inserted with hotkeys. Translation cached. Scale: 1k agents.
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for chats, messages, cans, translation cache.\n2. Translation: API call cached by (text_hash, target_lang).

database_design.queries: Translate or cache: SELECT FROM cache → if miss, API call → INSERT.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Cache write: ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.

race_conditions_addressed: Cache miss for hot text: API call may happen twice concurrently; INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING dedups.
Translation API down: fallback to original; flag for retry batch.
Canned with wrong vars: missing placeholders left empty; client validates.
scaling_considerations: 10x: translation cache hit-rate critical; LRU eviction policy.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: translate on every emit, no cache. Breaks: cost + latency.

title: Capstone: marketplace operations hub with order, payment, and dispute streams unified
business_context: Marketplace ops center: live unified feed of orders + payments + disputes + chargebacks across all merchants. Cross-stream correlation (dispute → original order). Scale: 1M orders/day, 500 ops users.
constraints: Each stream from different source (PG for orders, Mongo for support, Stripe webhooks for payments)
failure_modes_to_handle: Support event arrives before order known
architecture_reasoning: 1. Multiple source services each ship events to a Redis stream 'ops:events'. Worker normalizes + persists to PG (unified events) + emits via Socket.IO.\n2. Correlation: PG join on order_id when present.\n3. Filter compiled per-viewer; events pre-filtered before emit.

database_design.queries: History since: SELECT FROM ops_events WHERE ts > $since ORDER BY ts. Correlate: JOIN on order_id.

socket_io_design.rooms: ops:global, plus optional per-merchant filtered output via per-socket filter.

race_conditions_addressed: Cross-source out-of-order: events sorted by ts client-side; correlation by orderId works regardless of arrival order.
Support before order: support event persisted with orderId; later query joins as expected.
Filter explosion: server enforces filter cardinality; rejects empty filter for non-superusers.
Reconnect 1h gap: history fetch on subscribe with sinceTs.
scaling_considerations: 10x: per-viewer predicate compute scales with event rate × viewers — group by filter signature, evaluate once. 100x: stream-processing engine (Materialize) for true incremental view maintenance.
observability: Metrics: ingest_to_emit_p99 per source, filter_skip_rate, viewer_count, history_query_ms. Logs: every filter change with cardinality estimate. Alerts: per-source lag (orders > 5s, payments > 10s); viewer count drop spike (subscribe failures).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: just emit all events to all. Breaks: 1M events/day × 500 viewers = 500M emits, network saturated; no filter = ops user drowned in noise; no cross-source correlation = can't trace dispute back to order without manual join. Killer bug: a single misbehaving merchant generates 10k events/min; without per-merchant filter, every ops user's UI freezes.
